Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → Large language models and NLP → Fine-tuning and adaptation

Lesson 2 of 3

Fine-tuning and adaptation

Full fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, PEFT, and instruction tuning to adapt foundation models to specific domains and tasks.

30-40 min AI/ML flow Large language models and NLP 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Fine-tuning lets you build specialized models that outperform prompting alone on domain-specific tasks while controlling cost and latency.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. When to fine-tune Prompting first: try few-shot examples before committing to fine-tuning2. Parameter-efficient methods LoRA adds low-rank matrices to attention layers, training <1% of parameters3. Data preparation and evaluation High-quality instruction-response pairs matter more than dataset size

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Fine-tuning lets you build specialized models that outperform prompting alone on domain-specific tasks while controlling cost and latency. 2. When to fine-tune Prompting first: try few-shot examples before committing to fine-tuning3. Parameter-efficient methods LoRA adds low-rank matrices to attention layers, training <1% of parameters4. Data preparation and evaluation High-quality instruction-response pairs matter more than dataset size
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Fine-tuning lets you build specialized models that outperform prompting alone on domain-specific tasks while controlling cost and latency.

What to say first

  • Can decide when fine-tuning is worthwhile versus prompting strategies.
  • Understands LoRA mechanics and when to prefer it over full fine-tuning.
  • Knows how to prepare instruction-tuning datasets and evaluate results.
2

Study section

When to fine-tune

Fine-tuning is most valuable when the task requires domain-specific style, format adherence, or performance that prompting cannot achieve.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Prompting first: try few-shot examples before committing to fine-tuning
  • Fine-tuning wins when you need consistent output format or domain vocabulary
  • Cost-benefit: fine-tuned smaller models often beat prompted larger models at lower cost
3

Study section

Parameter-efficient methods

LoRA, QLoRA, and adapters update a tiny fraction of parameters while achieving near-full fine-tuning quality.

Call these points out explicitly

  • LoRA adds low-rank matrices to attention layers, training <1% of parameters
  • QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization with LoRA for memory efficiency
  • Adapter layers insert small trainable modules between frozen transformer blocks

Code example

Fine-tune with LoRA using PEFT

from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

# Load base model
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")

# Configure LoRA: low-rank adapters on attention layers
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=8,                  # rank of the update matrices
    lora_alpha=32,        # scaling factor
    lora_dropout=0.1,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],  # only adapt Q and V
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Output: "trainable params: 4,194,304 || all params: 6,742,609,920 || 0.06%"
4

Study section

Data preparation and evaluation

Instruction formatting, data quality, evaluation metrics, and avoiding catastrophic forgetting are critical to successful fine-tuning.

Call these points out explicitly

  • High-quality instruction-response pairs matter more than dataset size
  • Evaluation should include both automated metrics and human preference ratings
  • Mixing general data prevents catastrophic forgetting of base capabilities

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can decide when fine-tuning is worthwhile versus prompting strategies.
  • Understands LoRA mechanics and when to prefer it over full fine-tuning.
  • Knows how to prepare instruction-tuning datasets and evaluate results.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Fine-tuning without first exhausting prompt engineering approaches.
  • Using low-quality or biased training data that degrades model behavior.
  • Evaluating only on automated metrics without checking output quality manually.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Explain how LoRA achieves parameter efficiency. What are its limitations?
  • How would you prepare a dataset for instruction-tuning a customer support model?
  • What is catastrophic forgetting and how do you prevent it during fine-tuning?

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can decide when fine-tuning is worthwhile versus prompting strategies.
  • Understands LoRA mechanics and when to prefer it over full fine-tuning.
  • Knows how to prepare instruction-tuning datasets and evaluate results.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Fine-tuning without first exhausting prompt engineering approaches.
  • Using low-quality or biased training data that degrades model behavior.
  • Evaluating only on automated metrics without checking output quality manually.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • Explain how LoRA achieves parameter efficiency. What are its limitations?
  • How would you prepare a dataset for instruction-tuning a customer support model?
  • What is catastrophic forgetting and how do you prevent it during fine-tuning?

Code example

Implement LoRA from scratch

advanced coding

Implement a simplified LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) layer in PyTorch. Show how it reduces trainable parameters while preserving model quality.

What to notice

  • Freeze original weights with requires_grad_(False)
  • B initialized to zeros means LoRA output starts at zero (preserves original behavior)
  • A has shape (rank, in_features), B has shape (out_features, rank)
  • Total LoRA params = rank * (in_features + out_features)

Expected outcome

LoRA adds ~2% trainable params for rank=8 on a 768x768 layer

Starter code 
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LoRALayer(nn.Module):
    """Low-Rank Adaptation layer.
    Wraps an existing linear layer and adds low-rank update: output = Wx + BAx
    where B is (out_features, rank) and A is (rank, in_features)
    """
    def __init__(self, original_layer, rank=4):
        super().__init__()
        # TODO: Store original layer (frozen) and create A, B matrices
        # A should be initialized with small random values
        # B should be initialized to zeros (so LoRA starts as identity)
        pass

    def forward(self, x):
        # TODO: Return original output + low-rank update
        pass

# Test: wrap a large linear layer
original = nn.Linear(768, 768)
lora = LoRALayer(original, rank=8)
original_params = sum(p.numel() for p in original.parameters())
lora_params = sum(p.numel() for p in lora.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"Original params: {original_params}")
print(f"LoRA trainable params: {lora_params}")
print(f"Reduction: {lora_params/original_params*100:.1f}%")
Reference solution 
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LoRALayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, original_layer, rank=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.original = original_layer
        self.original.weight.requires_grad_(False)
        if self.original.bias is not None:
            self.original.bias.requires_grad_(False)
        in_features = original_layer.in_features
        out_features = original_layer.out_features
        self.A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(rank, in_features) * 0.01)
        self.B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(out_features, rank))

    def forward(self, x):
        original_output = self.original(x)
        lora_output = (x @ self.A.T) @ self.B.T
        return original_output + lora_output

Code example

Prepare instruction-tuning dataset

intermediate coding

Transform raw customer support conversations into instruction-tuning format (system/user/assistant). Implement quality filters and train/eval split.

What to notice

  • Instruction format uses system/user/assistant message roles
  • Filter for quality: resolved conversations, minimum response length
  • JSONL format: one JSON object per line

Expected outcome

Properly formatted JSONL with system prompts, filtered for quality

Starter code 
import json

# Raw support conversations
raw_data = [
    {"customer": "My order #1234 hasn't arrived", "agent": "I can see order #1234 was shipped on Monday. Let me check the tracking status for you.", "resolved": True},
    {"customer": "How do I return this?", "agent": "You can initiate a return from your order history page. Would you like me to walk you through it?", "resolved": True},
    {"customer": "This is terrible!!!", "agent": "I'm sorry to hear that. I want to help resolve this. Can you tell me more about what went wrong?", "resolved": False},
]

# TODO: Convert to instruction format
# TODO: Add quality filters (resolved conversations only, min length, etc.)
# TODO: Create 80/20 train/eval split
# TODO: Save as JSONL
Reference solution 
import json
import random

raw_data = [
    {"customer": "My order #1234 hasn't arrived", "agent": "I can see order #1234 was shipped on Monday. Let me check the tracking status for you.", "resolved": True},
    {"customer": "How do I return this?", "agent": "You can initiate a return from your order history page. Would you like me to walk you through it?", "resolved": True},
    {"customer": "This is terrible!!!", "agent": "I'm sorry to hear that. I want to help resolve this. Can you tell me more about what went wrong?", "resolved": False},
]

def to_instruction_format(conv):
    return {
        "messages": [
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful customer support agent. Be empathetic, professional, and solution-oriented."},
            {"role": "user", "content": conv["customer"]},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": conv["agent"]}
        ]
    }

# Quality filters
filtered = [c for c in raw_data if c["resolved"] and len(c["agent"]) > 20]
formatted = [to_instruction_format(c) for c in filtered]

# Split
random.shuffle(formatted)
split_idx = int(len(formatted) * 0.8)
train, eval_set = formatted[:split_idx], formatted[split_idx:]
print(f"Train: {len(train)}, Eval: {len(eval_set)}")

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "Fine-tuning and adaptation" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • Explain how LoRA achieves parameter efficiency. What are its limitations?
  • When to fine-tune
  • Parameter-efficient methods
  • Can decide when fine-tuning is worthwhile versus prompting strategies.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
